GenPref × Signed Gap & Preference — Results Only (No Refitting)

2026-03-18

Overview

The central question of this analysis is whether a model’s generic preference for one word over another — independent of its corpus-level frequency — shapes how language models represent those words over the course of training. We examine two primary outcome variables (signed gap and raw preference) and one secondary outcome (absolute signed gap), each approached with three complementary analyses.

Variable definitions

The raw inputs at each checkpoint are: alpha_logprob and nonalpha_logprob (the model’s log-probabilities for the alpha and non-alpha form of a binomial pair), and alpha_seen, beta_seen (cumulative counts of how many times each form appeared in the training corpus up to and including that checkpoint — these are checkpoint-specific, not whole-corpus statistics).

# Outcomes
preference     <- alpha_logprob - nonalpha_logprob   # model log-prob preference for alpha
log_freq_ratio <- log(alpha_seen / beta_seen)         # log corpus frequency ratio at this checkpoint
signed_gap     <- preference - log_freq_ratio         # deviation from corpus-predicted preference
abs_signed_gap <- abs(signed_gap)                     # magnitude of deviation, direction-free

signed_gap is the primary frequency-corrected outcome. A value of zero means the model’s preference for alpha over beta exactly matches what the corpus frequency ratio predicts. Positive values indicate the model over-favors alpha relative to frequency; negative values indicate under-favoring. abs_signed_gap removes direction and asks only about the size of the deviation. preference is the raw outcome used in Part II, with frequency included as a predictor rather than subtracted.

The predictors used in all regression models are:

freq_prob_c <- plogis(log_freq_ratio) - 0.5          # P(alpha in corpus) - 0.5; range ~ (-0.5, 0.5)
genpref_c   <- genpref - mean(genpref)                # GenPref score, centered at sample mean
log_total_c <- scale(log(alpha_seen + beta_seen))     # log total exposures, z-scored (scope varies)
tokens_c    <- scale(tokens)                          # training token count, z-scored within model

freq_prob_c transforms log_freq_ratio to a probability scale via the logistic function and centers it at 0, so that 0 represents equal corpus frequency for alpha and beta, and ±0.5 represent the extremes. genpref_c is the human generic preference score (from a separate behavioural experiment), centered at the sample mean so that the intercept represents an item with average generic preference. Both freq_prob_c and log_total_c are checkpoint-specific: they are derived from alpha_seen and beta_seen, which accumulate over training.

Z-scoring scope for log_total_c varies across analyses and materially affects interpretation. Because total corpus exposure grows monotonically with training, z-scoring log_total_c globally would confound two distinct sources of variation: between-item differences (some binomials simply appear more in the corpus) and between-checkpoint differences (later checkpoints have seen more tokens overall). The analyses handle this as follows:

  • Analyses 1, 2, 4, 5, and Part III: log_total_c is z-scored within each checkpoint’s items separately, so it captures only between-item exposure differences at a fixed training stage. This keeps log_total_c approximately orthogonal to training stage.
  • Analyses 3 and 6: log_total_c is z-scored across all checkpoints pooled within a model. Here it carries both between-item and some between-checkpoint variation; tokens_c is included in the same model to account for the training-stage component.

Bayesian model settings

All models are Bayesian linear mixed-effects regressions fit with brms (backend: cmdstanr). Priors: Normal(0, 1) on all fixed effects and the intercept; Exponential(1) on all SD parameters (random effects and residual sigma). Each model ran for 6000 iterations with 3000 warmup. All models include a by-item random intercept (1 | binom) to account for baseline differences between binomial pairs. Credible intervals are 95% posterior credible intervals; asterisks (*) in plots mark intervals that exclude zero.

Analysis structure

Each of the two primary outcomes (signed gap, preference) is examined three ways: a single model at the final checkpoint (Analyses 1, 4); separate models at 20 evenly-spaced checkpoints to track coefficient trajectories (Analyses 2, 5); and a single pooled model with training stage as a continuous predictor interacted with all other predictors (Analyses 3, 6). Part III models absolute signed gap as a function of training stage and item-level frequency.

Discover fitted models

Descriptive: Signed Gap Over Training

Before examining regression results, these plots summarise signed_gap descriptively at each of the 20 sampled checkpoints — no conditioning on predictors. The summaries are computed across items within each checkpoint from the stored trajectory fit data (fit$data). Because brms strips non-formula columns before saving, preference and log_freq_ratio are recovered as:

log_freq_ratio <- qlogis(freq_prob_c + 0.5)    # inverse of plogis(log_freq_ratio) - 0.5
preference     <- signed_gap + log_freq_ratio   # signed_gap = preference - log_freq_ratio

Three summaries are plotted. Mean signed gap (D1) shows whether models have a systematic directional bias — over- or under-favoring alpha relative to corpus frequency on average. A mean of zero would indicate that, averaged across items, preferences match corpus statistics exactly; non-zero values indicate a systematic offset in one direction. Mean absolute signed gap (D2) discards direction and tracks only the size of deviations, asking whether models become more or less extreme over training. Preference–frequency correlation (D3) computes the Pearson correlation between preference and log_freq_ratio across items at each checkpoint, measuring how well the model’s preference ordering across items tracks the corpus frequency ratio — without penalising for overall scale differences.

Plot D1: Mean Signed Gap

X axis: training tokens (millions). Y axis: mean of signed_gap = preference - log_freq_ratio across all items at that checkpoint, with 95% CI of the mean. A value of zero means the model’s preferences match corpus frequency predictions on average; positive = systematic over-favoring of alpha; negative = systematic under-favoring.

Plot D2: Mean Absolute Signed Gap

X axis: training tokens (millions). Y axis: mean of |signed_gap| across items, with 95% CI. This is scale-sensitive: a model that has learned the correct frequency ordering but amplifies its preferences beyond the frequency signal will show a large mean |signed gap| even if its Pearson r with log_freq_ratio is high (Plot D3). Increasing values over training indicate that models’ preference magnitudes grow faster than the frequency baseline, not necessarily that frequency tracking is worsening.

Plot D3: Preference–Frequency Correlation Over Training

X axis: training tokens (millions). Y axis: Pearson r between preference and log_freq_ratio = log(alpha_seen / beta_seen) computed across items at each checkpoint. This is scale-insensitive: r = 1 means the model ranks items by preference in the same order as the corpus frequency ratio, regardless of absolute magnitude. Increasing r over training indicates improving frequency-tracking accuracy in terms of ordering, even if D2 also increases (scale amplification and ordering accuracy can both improve simultaneously).

Part III — Absolute Signed Gap

Motivation: Plots D2 and D3 reveal an apparent paradox — the mean absolute signed gap grows over training (models’ preferences become more extreme relative to the frequency baseline), while the preference–frequency correlation also improves (models better track the relative ordering of corpus frequencies across items). These two facts are compatible: a model can correctly rank items by frequency while scaling its preferences more aggressively than the frequency ratio warrants. This analysis tests both phenomena jointly in a regression framework.

Data: all available checkpoints for each model, with preference averaged over the 51 prompts per (binomial, checkpoint). Outcome:

abs_signed_gap <- abs(preference - log_freq_ratio)

Predictors and their transformations:

tokens_c    <- scale(tokens)                          # z-scored within model's full token range
log_total_c <- scale(log(alpha_seen + beta_seen))     # z-scored WITHIN each checkpoint's items

tokens_c captures training stage. log_total_c is z-scored within each checkpoint (same approach as Analyses 2 and 5) to keep the two predictors approximately orthogonal: because total corpus exposure grows monotonically with training, global z-scoring would confound between-item and between-checkpoint variation. Within-checkpoint z-scoring ensures log_total_c reflects only between-item differences in exposure at each training stage. The model is:

\[\text{abs\_signed\_gap} \sim \text{tokens\_c} \times \text{log\_total\_c} + (1 \mid \text{binom})\]

The interaction tests whether the effect of item-level exposure on gap magnitude changes linearly over the course of training (e.g., whether high-frequency items show disproportionately growing gaps). The main effect of tokens_c captures the overall growth (or shrinkage) of gap magnitude with training; the main effect of log_total_c captures whether more-exposed items show larger or smaller gaps at any given training stage.

Plot P1: Coefficients

Fixed-effect posterior means and 95% credible intervals for tokens_c, log_total_c, and their interaction, one fit per model-slug. Asterisks mark intervals excluding zero.

Table P1: Coefficient Summary

Posterior means, standard errors, and 95% credible intervals for all fixed effects across models.

Posterior fixed effects: abs_signed_gap ~ tokens_c * log_total_c + (1|binom). Mean = posterior mean; SD = posterior SD; 2.5%/97.5% = 95% credible interval bounds.

Part I — Signed Gap

The signed gap (signed_gap = preference - log_freq_ratio) is the primary outcome. By subtracting the corpus frequency log-ratio from the model’s log-probability preference, it isolates the portion of the model’s preference that cannot be explained by how often each form appears in the training data. A positive coefficient for any predictor means that predictor is associated with the model preferring alpha more than corpus frequency alone predicts; a negative coefficient means less than predicted. This is the cleanest test of whether something beyond frequency — including generic human preferences — shapes the model’s internal representations.

Analysis 1: Signed Gap — Final Checkpoint

Data: all prompt-level rows at the model’s final training checkpoint (51 prompts per binomial item, ~36,000 rows per model). Outcome: signed_gap = preference - log_freq_ratio. Predictors: genpref_c (GenPref centered at sample mean), freq_prob_c (= plogis(log_freq_ratio) - 0.5, bounded to ~(-0.5, 0.5)), log_total_c (= scale(log(alpha_seen + beta_seen)), z-scored across the final checkpoint’s items). The model is:

\[\text{signed\_gap} \sim \text{genpref\_c} \times \text{log\_total\_c} + \text{freq\_prob\_c} \times \text{log\_total\_c} + (1 \mid \text{binom})\]

The two-way interactions with log_total_c allow the effects of GenPref and relative frequency to vary with how much total exposure the model has had to each item pair. This is motivated by the expectation that frequency-driven effects may be stronger for items seen more often, and that sensitivity to human preferences may develop gradually through exposure. Because data comes from only the final checkpoint, log_total_c captures only between-item variation in total corpus frequency (i.e., some binomials just appear more often). Asterisks mark credible intervals that exclude zero.

Posterior fixed effects: signed_gap ~ genpref_c * log_total_c + freq_prob_c * log_total_c + (1|binom). Mean = posterior mean; SD = posterior SD; 2.5%/97.5% = 95% credible interval bounds.

Analysis 2: Signed Gap — Per-Checkpoint Trajectory

Data: 20 evenly-spaced checkpoints sampled by index across each model’s full checkpoint sequence (so spacing in tokens is approximately but not exactly uniform). Each checkpoint is modelled separately using only rows from that checkpoint; prompt-level data are retained (51 prompts per binomial). Outcome: signed_gap. Model formula (fit independently at each checkpoint):

\[\text{signed\_gap} \sim \text{genpref\_c} \times \text{log\_total\_c} + \text{freq\_prob\_c} \times \text{log\_total\_c} + (1 \mid \text{binom})\]

Critical z-scoring decision: log_total_c = scale(log(alpha_seen + beta_seen)) is z-scored within each checkpoint’s items — not globally across checkpoints. This is essential because log(alpha_seen + beta_seen) grows monotonically with training (later checkpoints have seen more tokens), so global z-scoring would conflate between-item variation (some binomials simply occur more in the corpus) with training-stage variation. By z-scoring within each checkpoint, log_total_c captures only the between-item component: at any given training stage, how much more (or less) has this item pair been seen relative to other pairs? Each coefficient is therefore interpretable as a within-checkpoint effect, and the trajectory of that coefficient across checkpoints tracks how the effect evolves over training.

Plot 2a shows the raw log(total_seen) mean ± SD and item counts across checkpoints as a sanity check on coverage and the rationale for within-checkpoint z-scoring. Plot 2b shows coefficient trajectories. Plot 2c focuses on the GenPref coefficient, showing P(β > 0) — approximated as pnorm(0, mean = estimate, sd = se, lower.tail = FALSE) using the posterior mean and SD — at each checkpoint.

Plot 2a: Predictor Statistics Over Training

Plot 2b: Coefficients Over Training

Plot 2c: GenPref Significance Over Training

Analysis 3: Signed Gap — Checkpoint-Interacted

Data: all available checkpoints for each model, with preference averaged over the 51 prompts per (binomial, checkpoint) before modelling (reducing the data to one row per binomial × checkpoint to keep the dataset within Stan’s memory limits). Outcome: signed_gap computed on the prompt-averaged preference. Predictors: genpref_c, freq_prob_c, log_total_c, and tokens_c (= scale(tokens), z-scored within each model’s full token range). The model is:

\[\text{signed\_gap} \sim (\text{genpref\_c} + \text{freq\_prob\_c} + \text{log\_total\_c}) \times \text{tokens\_c} + (1 \mid \text{binom})\]

Z-scoring note: in this analysis, log_total_c = scale(log(alpha_seen + beta_seen)) is z-scored across all checkpoints pooled within a model, not within each checkpoint. This differs from Analysis 2. As a result, log_total_c here carries both between-item variation (some binomials appear more in the corpus) and some between-checkpoint variation (items seen in later checkpoints will tend to have higher total exposure). The tokens_c predictor is included to account for the training-stage component, but the two predictors are not fully orthogonal in this analysis. The interactions with tokens_c estimate whether each predictor’s effect changes linearly with training stage — the key tradeoff relative to Analysis 2, which makes no linearity assumption but cannot directly estimate or test the training-stage interaction.

Plot 3a: Coefficients

Posterior fixed effects: signed_gap ~ (genpref_c + freq_prob_c + log_total_c) * tokens_c + (1|binom).

Part II — Preference

Analyses 4–6 mirror Analyses 1–3 but use raw preference as the outcome rather than signed gap. Whereas signed gap isolates the portion of the model’s preference that goes beyond what corpus frequency predicts, raw preference conflates frequency-driven and frequency-independent effects. Including freq_prob_c as a predictor in the preference models controls for relative frequency statistically rather than subtracting it from the outcome, which makes the interpretation of the GenPref coefficient subtly different: here, it reflects the effect of GenPref on preference holding relative frequency constant, rather than the effect on the frequency-corrected residual.

The two outcome measures are therefore complementary. If GenPref predicts signed gap, it means generic human preferences shape the model’s representations above and beyond what frequency can explain. If GenPref additionally predicts raw preference, it may be partly because GenPref and relative corpus frequency are correlated — words that humans generically prefer may also tend to appear more often in the corpus. Comparing the two sets of results helps to disentangle these possibilities.

Analysis 4: Preference — Final Checkpoint

Data: all prompt-level rows at the model’s final training checkpoint (51 prompts per binomial item, ~36,000 rows per model). Outcome: raw preference = alpha_logprob - nonalpha_logprob, the model’s log-probability difference between the alpha and non-alpha form of each binomial pair. Predictors: genpref_c (GenPref centered at sample mean), freq_prob_c (= plogis(log_freq_ratio) - 0.5), log_total_c (= scale(log(alpha_seen + beta_seen)), z-scored within the final checkpoint’s items). The model is:

\[\text{preference} \sim \text{genpref\_c} \times \text{log\_total\_c} + \text{freq\_prob\_c} \times \text{log\_total\_c} + (1 \mid \text{binom})\]

This is structurally identical to Analysis 1, but the outcome is raw preference rather than signed gap. Corpus frequency is not subtracted from the outcome; instead, freq_prob_c enters as a predictor, controlling for relative frequency statistically. The genpref_c coefficient therefore reflects GenPref’s contribution to preference holding relative corpus frequency constant, rather than its effect on the frequency-corrected residual. A positive genpref_c coefficient here could arise either because human generic preferences genuinely shape model representations, or because GenPref and corpus frequency are correlated (words humans prefer generically may also appear more often). Comparing this result to Analysis 1 — where frequency is subtracted from the outcome — helps distinguish those possibilities: if the GenPref effect is attenuated in Analysis 1 relative to Analysis 4, it suggests that part of the Analysis 4 effect is mediated by corpus frequency.

Posterior fixed effects: preference ~ genpref_c * log_total_c + freq_prob_c * log_total_c + (1|binom).

Analysis 5: Preference — Per-Checkpoint Trajectory

Data: 20 evenly-spaced checkpoints sampled by index across each model’s full checkpoint sequence, modelled separately using prompt-level data (51 prompts per binomial). Outcome: raw preference = alpha_logprob - nonalpha_logprob. Predictors: genpref_c, freq_prob_c, log_total_c (z-scored within each checkpoint’s items). Model formula fit independently at each checkpoint:

\[\text{preference} \sim \text{genpref\_c} \times \text{log\_total\_c} + \text{freq\_prob\_c} \times \text{log\_total\_c} + (1 \mid \text{binom})\]

The z-scoring rationale is the same as Analysis 2: within-checkpoint z-scoring of log_total_c keeps between-item and between-checkpoint variation separate, so each coefficient reflects a within-checkpoint effect. The key interpretive difference from Analysis 2: because signed_gap has already subtracted the corpus frequency signal from the outcome, the freq_prob_c coefficient in Analysis 2 reflects only residual frequency sensitivity beyond that correction. Here, freq_prob_c absorbs the direct frequency effect as well — so its coefficient will typically be larger — and the GenPref coefficient reflects GenPref’s contribution to raw preference after statistically controlling for corpus frequency, rather than after removing it from the outcome.

Plot 5a: Predictor Statistics Over Training

Plot 5b: Coefficients Over Training

Plot 5c: GenPref Significance Over Training

Analysis 6: Preference — Checkpoint-Interacted

Data: all available checkpoints for each model, with preference averaged over the 51 prompts per (binomial, checkpoint). Outcome: raw preference = alpha_logprob - nonalpha_logprob. Predictors: genpref_c, freq_prob_c, log_total_c, and tokens_c (= scale(tokens), z-scored within each model’s full token range). The model is:

\[\text{preference} \sim (\text{genpref\_c} + \text{freq\_prob\_c} + \text{log\_total\_c}) \times \text{tokens\_c} + (1 \mid \text{binom})\]

This is the counterpart of Analysis 3, but for raw preference rather than signed gap. The interactions with tokens_c test whether each predictor’s effect on raw preference changes linearly with training stage.

Z-scoring note: log_total_c is z-scored across all checkpoints pooled within a model (same approach as Analysis 3, different from Analysis 5). It therefore carries both between-item and some between-checkpoint variation; tokens_c is included to partial out the training-stage component, but the two predictors are not fully orthogonal. Comparing the genpref_c:tokens_c interaction between Analyses 3 and 6 indicates whether any training-stage change in GenPref sensitivity is specific to the frequency-corrected signal, or is present in the raw preference as well.

Plot 6a: Coefficients

Posterior fixed effects: preference ~ (genpref_c + freq_prob_c + log_total_c) * tokens_c + (1|binom).
